---
title: "Safe bench practice and trustworthy measurement"
description: "Control electrical hazards, reason in SI units, and account for measurement intrusion during current-limited, extra-low-voltage bench work."
chapter-id: F01
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This chapter presents a complete instructional path: exposition, worked examples,
an executable check, a reconciliation with data, and a full exercise set. It is
still subject to technical and editorial revision, and the physical evidence it
describes is collected in the aligned laboratory work, not in the prose. See how
to interpret status levels in the [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd).
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for this chapter"}
Everything here is written for **current-limited, extra-low-voltage** bench work
under local supervision and approved procedures. Reading this chapter is **not**
authorization to work on mains, high-voltage, high-energy battery, RF-power,
rotating-machine, medical, or any safety-critical system. When the evidence or
the safety boundary is inadequate, the correct engineering action is to
**stop** [@iec61010; @nfpa70e].
:::

## Central question

> How do you investigate an electrical system without creating a new hazard or
> corrupting the evidence?

Two kinds of failure can occur at the bench. The first is **physical**: the
circuit injures someone, damages an instrument, or destroys the part under test.
The second is **evidential**: the measurement changes the circuit, but we treat
the displayed number as though it described the undisturbed system. Safe,
trustworthy work requires us to control both failures before we touch a probe to
the circuit.

Picture the situation concretely. A small development board is in front of you,
a bench supply on its left, a digital multimeter on its right. A colleague says a
node "should be about 3.3 volts." The temptation is to set the supply, clip the
probes, and read the display. This chapter argues that three questions come
*before* the probe touches metal, and that skipping them is what separates a
result from a guess:

1. **What energy is available, and where can it go?** (So that energizing is a
   staged, reversible act, not a gamble.)
2. **In what units am I reasoning, and does my estimate even have the right
   dimensions and order of magnitude?** (So that a wrong answer is caught by
   arithmetic before it is caught by smoke.)
3. **How much will my instrument disturb the thing I am trying to observe, and
   how well do I actually know the number it returns?** (So that the reading is
   evidence, not an artifact.)

::: {.callout-tip title="Make a prediction first"}
Before reading on, commit to two directional answers. This is a calculation, not
an instruction to make body contact. **(a)** If both terminals of a 9 V battery
were connected through an illustrative dry-finger path, would the current be
microamps, milliamps, or amps? **(b)** If you measure a node in a 1 MΩ resistive
divider with an ordinary multimeter, will the reading be too high, too low, or
exactly right? Write the answers down. We reconcile them with a model and with
data later in the chapter; the point of committing now is that a surprise you
predicted teaches far more than one you merely observed [@horowitz2015art].
:::

## Learning outcomes

After completing this chapter — and it assumes no prior circuit theory, only
comfort with algebra, ratios, and scientific notation
([M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd)) — you should be able to:

- **describe an electrical hazard as a source–path–target system** and explain
  why an approved bench boundary must constrain current, duration, and available
  energy rather than relying on a voltage label alone [@iec60479];
- **energize a circuit in reversible stages** using a bench supply's voltage and
  current limits, and state explicit *stop conditions* before applying power
  (builds on the energy model above);
- **reason quantitatively in SI**, carrying units through a calculation,
  checking that both sides of an equation have compatible units, and reporting
  only as many significant figures as the evidence supports
  [@bipm2019si; @nist811];
- **distinguish equality, approximation, tolerance, bound, and uncertainty**,
  and attach a defensible uncertainty to a measured value [@jcgm2008gum;
  @jcgm2012vim];
- **predict how an instrument loads a circuit**, estimate the resulting error,
  and decide whether a reading is trustworthy evidence or an artifact of the
  measurement (builds on the SI and uncertainty ideas above); and
- **keep a traceable engineering record** — configuration, raw data, and
  reasoning — that another person could use to reproduce your result
  ([T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd)).

This chapter has no single equation to memorize. What it offers instead is a way
of standing in front of a bench, built from three small, sturdy ideas. Each one
answers one of the three questions above, and each recurs, scaled up, in every
later part of the book.

## Energy paths and hazard control

An electrical **hazard** is energy that can reach a target along a path. We can
control the hazard by limiting the source, interrupting the path, or protecting
the target. The diagram below separates those three parts. Under each part, it
shows a control that weakens or removes the energy path.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-hazard-model
%%| fig-cap: "The energy–path–target view of an electrical hazard, stacked source to target. Limiting or removing any one link controls the risk; the rounded nodes name the control available at each stage. Typical instances: a *source* is a bench supply, a charged capacitor, or a cell; a *path* is a probe tip, wet skin, a solder bridge, a dropped tool, an arc, or a shared return; a *target* is you, the device under test, or a meter."
%%| fig-alt: "Three stages stacked vertically — an energy source, then a coupling path, then a target — each with a control action branching to the side, showing that limiting or removing any one link controls the hazard."
flowchart TB
  S["Energy source"]
  P["Path<br/>couples source to target"]
  T["Target"]
  S -->|drives current| P
  P -->|delivers energy| T
  SC(["Control: limit V and I;<br/>discharge; isolate"])
  PC(["Control: insulate;<br/>one hand back;<br/>manage the return"])
  TC(["Control: shielding, PPE,<br/>distance; a bounded claim"])
  S -.-> SC
  P -.-> PC
  T -.-> TC
```

Two facts turn this picture into engineering judgment.

**It is current through the body, for a duration, that harms — not voltage in the
abstract.** Voltage is what *drives* current, but the injury is done by the
current that actually flows, its path and frequency, and how long it flows. The
canonical reference data [@iec60479] supports the order-of-magnitude screening
bands in @tbl-body-current for 50/60 Hz current through an adult. They are not
universal thresholds for an individual:

| Body current (AC, 50/60 Hz) | Typical effect |
|---|---|
| ~1 mA | threshold of perception — a faint tingle |
| several to tens of mA | painful contraction and impaired voluntary release become possible |
| tens of mA, sustained | the probability of dangerous cardiac effects rises with current and duration |
| hundreds of mA | severe cardiac and thermal injury is possible even over short intervals |

: Approximate screening bands for 50/60 Hz current through an adult body. Effects depend strongly on magnitude, duration, path, frequency, contact conditions, and individual physiology; use the governing safety standard rather than this table for protection design [@iec60479]. {#tbl-body-current}

Protection requirements therefore use specified combinations of voltage,
current, duration, insulation, disconnection, and environment. The physiological
table explains why current matters; it does not by itself select a protective
device or trip rating.

**Whether a given voltage is dangerous depends on the complete current path.**
The body is not a fixed resistor: its impedance depends on touch voltage,
duration, frequency, current path, skin moisture, contact area, pressure, and
temperature [@iec60479]. A deliberately simplified resistive screen can still
give an order of magnitude when its assumed path resistance is stated. In that
screen the body completes a circuit from an exposed contact back to the source
(@fig-shock-path). Using Ohm's law (developed in
[F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd)) with an **illustrative** dry-contact
value of 50 kΩ and a 9 V source gives

$$
I \approx \frac{V}{R} = \frac{9~\text{V}}{50\times10^{3}~\Omega}
      = 1.8\times10^{-4}~\text{A} = 0.18~\text{mA}.
$$

![A first-order resistive screen for a contact path. The body completes a path from an exposed contact back to the source; $R_\text{body}$ is an illustrative equivalent for stated conditions, not a constant physiological resistance.](../../../build/circuits/f01-shock-path.png){#fig-shock-path fig-alt="A voltage source V_S connects earth to an exposed live contact; the body, represented by an illustrative equivalent resistance R_body, returns from the contact to earth, and contact current I flows through the path."}

The result is below the approximate perception scale in @tbl-body-current, but it
does not establish a generally safe touch voltage: the assumed impedance is both
condition-dependent and voltage-dependent. Replacing the source and contact
conditions by a much higher voltage and a much lower path impedance changes the
result by orders of magnitude. The lesson is that **safety is a claim about the
whole source–path–target system**, not a voltage label. Practical work in Part I
therefore uses an approved, current-limited source no higher than the stated lab
limit, never intentional body contact, and stop conditions fixed before power.

::: {.callout-important title="Current limiting bounds sustained fault current"}
A bench power supply has two knobs, and the second one is the safety feature. Set
the **voltage limit** to the value the circuit needs; set the **current limit**
(sometimes labelled *I~limit~*) to just above the current the
healthy circuit should draw. On a sustained low-resistance fault, the control
loop leaves constant-voltage mode, reduces its output, and indicates CC or
current-limit operation. The specified limit bounds the regulated steady fault
current; output capacitance, wiring inductance, control-loop response, and
transient overshoot can still deliver energy before regulation settles. Current
limiting is therefore a central low-voltage bench control, not a substitute for
bounding stored energy, contact heating, and the complete fault path.
:::

**Stored energy remains after switching off.** "Powered off" is not "safe." A capacitor
holds energy $E=\tfrac12 C V^2$ after the supply is disconnected, and it will
deliver that energy into the first low-resistance path it finds — including a
finger or a shorting screwdriver. A de-energized board is only genuinely
de-energized once its storage elements are *proven* discharged. Installation
practice uses a five-step de-energizing sequence [@en50110]:

1. Disconnect the source completely.
2. Secure the source against reconnection.
3. Verify the absence of voltage with a tester whose operation you have checked.
4. Discharge stored energy and, where required, connect it to ground.
5. Protect the work area from anything nearby that remains energized.

At an extra-low-voltage teaching bench, we apply the same logic: disconnect,
wait for the stated discharge time, and measure before touching. We verify the
absence of voltage instead of assuming that the switch removed every energy
source.

**Authorization and stop conditions come before power, not after trouble.** Two
sentences, written in your notebook before you energize, prevent most bench
incidents:

- *Authorization:* "I am approved to do **this specific work** at **this energy
  level** using **this apparatus**." A chapter, a datasheet, or a forum post is
  never authorization; a supervisor, a lab rule, or a signed procedure is.
- *Stop conditions:* the pre-declared observations that end the experiment
  immediately — smoke, odor, discoloration, an unexpected sound, unplanned rapid
  temperature rise, a current limit that trips, a reading outside the predicted
  range, or the boundary of the authorized work.

## SI units and quantitative discipline

The second control is disciplined arithmetic. Engineering uses the
International System of Units (SI). Carrying those units through a calculation
helps us catch an error before the circuit exposes it.

Seven base units define everything else [@bipm2019si]:

| Base quantity | Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| time | second | s |
| length | metre | m |
| mass | kilogram | kg |
| **electric current** | **ampere** | **A** |
| thermodynamic temperature | kelvin | K |
| amount of substance | mole | mol |
| luminous intensity | candela | cd |

The ampere is the electrical base unit, and since the 2019 redefinition it is
*defined* by fixing the elementary charge at exactly
$e = 1.602176634\times10^{-19}~\text{C}$ — so the coulomb, the volt, the ohm,
and every quantity in this book trace back to a fundamental constant rather than
to a physical artifact in a vault [@bipm2019si]. The electrical units you will
use constantly are all *derived* from the seven:

| Quantity | Unit | Symbol | In base units |
|---|---|---|---|
| charge | coulomb | C | A·s |
| voltage (potential difference) | volt | V | kg·m²·s⁻³·A⁻¹ |
| resistance | ohm | Ω | V/A |
| capacitance | farad | F | C/V |
| power | watt | W | J/s = V·A |
| energy | joule | J | W·s |
| frequency | hertz | Hz | $s^-1$ |

Real quantities span an enormous dynamic range, so we scale with **prefixes**
rather than trailing zeros. The ones you will meet daily:

| Factor | 10⁻¹² | 10⁻⁹ | 10⁻⁶ | 10⁻³ | 10³ | 10⁶ | 10⁹ | 10¹² |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prefix | pico | nano | micro | milli | kilo | mega | giga | tera |
| Symbol | p | n | µ | m | k | M | G | T |

Letter case changes the multiplier: **`m` is milli and `M` is mega**. They
differ by a factor of $10^{9}$. A 1 mΩ shunt and a 1 MΩ isolation resistor
therefore differ by a billion. Confusing the symbols changes the circuit, not
only its notation.

Three habits make units *do work* for you:

**Dimensional homogeneity** means that both sides of an equation, and every term
in a sum, have compatible units. This check can expose an algebra or unit
conversion error before we use the result. Is the time constant $\tau = RC$
really a time? Check:

$$
[\,R\,][\,C\,] = \Omega \cdot \text{F}
   = \frac{\text{V}}{\text{A}} \cdot \frac{\text{C}}{\text{V}}
   = \frac{\text{C}}{\text{A}}
   = \frac{\text{A}\cdot\text{s}}{\text{A}} = \text{s}. \qquad\checkmark
$$

If an $RC$ expression does not reduce to seconds, the derivation or its units
contain an error. The same check appears repeatedly in
[F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd), where $\tau=RC$ governs every
transient.

**Order-of-magnitude estimation.** Before any precise calculation, get the
exponent right. Will a common ¼ W resistor survive 5 V across 100 Ω?
$P = V^2/R = (5~\text{V})^2 / 100~\Omega = 0.25~\text{W}$ — *exactly* the rating,
so it runs at its thermal limit with no margin and you should choose a larger
part or a smaller voltage. A ten-second estimate prevented a scorched component;
[F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd) turns this reflex into a full power budget.

**Significant figures** should match the precision supported by the evidence. A
multimeter display of `4.286 V` shows millivolt resolution. If the measurement
uncertainty is ±0.05 V, the last two displayed digits are not supported. Report
`4.29 V ± 0.05 V` (or `4.29(5) V`) instead. **Resolution** is the smallest
displayed step. **Uncertainty** describes how widely values consistent with the
measurement may vary. The two quantities are not interchangeable.

::: {.callout-note title="Five words that are not synonyms"}
Precision of language is precision of thought. Keep these distinct, in prose and
in symbols [@jcgm2012vim]:

- **Equality (`=`)** — exact or by definition: $1~\text{k}\Omega = 1000~\Omega$.
- **Approximation (`≈`)** — a model result under stated assumptions:
  $I \approx V/R$ for an ideal resistor.
- **Tolerance (`±x%`)** — a manufacturing spread: a "100 Ω ±1%" resistor is
  guaranteed only to lie in [99, 101] Ω.
- **Bound (`≤`, `≥`)** — a one-sided guarantee: "dissipation $\le 0.25$ W."
- **Uncertainty (`± u`, coverage `k`)** — the dispersion of a *measured* value:
  "$V = (4.29 \pm 0.05)$ V, $k=2$" means an interval expected to contain the true
  value with ~95 % confidence [@jcgm2008gum].
:::

One more convention this book insists on from the start: **state what an
amplitude is**. "5 V" is ambiguous until you say whether it is DC, a peak, a
peak-to-peak swing, or an RMS value — a 5 V RMS sinusoid has a 7.07 V peak and a
14.14 V peak-to-peak span, and a fuse, a rating, or a shock hazard cares which
one you meant. In Part I the quantities are mostly DC. Labelling amplitude now
prepares us for the sinusoidal quantities in
[F08](f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd).

## Instrument loading and reference directions

The third control concerns **instrument loading**, the change an instrument
causes when we connect it to a circuit. An ideal voltmeter draws no current. A
real voltmeter has finite input resistance and draws some current. An ideal
oscilloscope probe adds nothing to the circuit. A real probe adds capacitance
and a return path.

The instrument therefore becomes part of the circuit under test. The reading
describes the joined circuit-plus-instrument system. In the diagram below, trace
the path from the node through the probe and instrument, then back through the
shared return. That closed loop explains why the instrument can change the
quantity it measures.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-measurement-loop
%%| fig-cap: "The measurement loop. The probe and the shared return connect the node of interest to an instrument of finite input impedance, so the quantity you read describes the joined circuit-plus-instrument loop, not the circuit alone."
%%| fig-alt: "The node of interest, characterized by its Thévenin resistance, connects through a probe to an instrument with finite input resistance and capacitance; the instrument returns to the node through a shared return, closing a loop, so the reading describes the combined loop rather than the circuit alone."
flowchart TB
  N["Node of interest<br/>Thévenin resistance Rₜₕ"]
  I["Instrument<br/>finite input Rᵢₙ ∥ Cᵢₙ"]
  N -->|probe| I
  I -->|shared return| N
  R(["What you read =<br/>the joined loop,<br/>not the circuit alone"])
  I -.-> R
```

The single most common intrusion is **loading**: a voltmeter of input resistance
$R_\text{in}$ placed across a node whose source (Thévenin) resistance is
$R_\text{th}$ forms a divider with it, and the fractional error in the reading is,
exactly within that linear, purely resistive equivalent,

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\,\frac{R_\text{th}}{R_\text{th} + R_\text{in}}.
$$ {#eq-loading}

![A voltmeter loads the node it measures. Its finite input resistance $R_\text{in}$ sits in parallel with $R_2$, so the value read at TP1 is the *loaded* divider — below the unloaded $V_S\,R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ by the fraction in @eq-loading, where $R_\text{th}=R_1\Vert R_2$ is the resistance the node presents to the meter.](../../../build/circuits/f01-divider-loading.png){#fig-divider-loading fig-alt="Voltage source V_S drives series resistors R1 and R2 to ground, with output test point TP1 at their junction; a voltmeter of input resistance R_in is connected from TP1 to the 0 V rail, in parallel with R2."}

The diagram and @eq-loading give two cases. When
$R_\text{in} \gg R_\text{th}$, loading is negligible. When
$R_\text{th}$ is comparable to $R_\text{in}$, the meter pulls the node voltage
down. This confirms the earlier prediction: measuring the 1 MΩ divider produces
a value that is too low because the loading term is negative.

The same reasoning applies to oscilloscope bandwidth, probe capacitance,
common-mode error, and ground loops. [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd)
develops those cases. For now, ask one question before using a reading: *How did
the instrument change this node?*

**Reference directions make a sign mean something.** A meter answers a question
you posed with your leads. Put the red lead on node A and black on node B and a
reading of $+4.29$ V means A is above B; swap the leads and the same physical
state reads $-4.29$ V. Neither is "wrong" — but only one matches the reference
direction you declared. Decide and write down the positive sense *before* you
probe, so that a negative result reads as "reality opposes my chosen reference,"
not "error." This convention is the whole subject of
[F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd); adopt it now as a habit.

### Bench instruments and their roles

The instruments are not a shopping list; each one is a lever on one of the three
ideas.

| Instrument | Primary role | Which idea it serves |
|---|---|---|
| **DC bench supply** | source energy with a *voltage* and a *current* limit | Energy — bound what a fault can deliver |
| **Digital multimeter (DMM)** | measure V, I, R, continuity | Units + intrusion — a number, with loading and uncertainty |
| **Oscilloscope + probe** | see a signal versus time | Intrusion — via bandwidth and probe capacitance |
| **Breadboard / test fixture** | hold the topology you can inspect | Energy — a return path you can see and reason about |
| **Engineering notebook** | record configuration, data, reasoning | All three — makes a result reproducible |

A breadboard deserves one specific warning about energy and the return path: its
long power rails have real resistance and inductance, its spring contacts are
intermittent, and its shared returns invite ground loops. Inspect the topology
and the return path *de-energized* — trace where current will actually flow —
before you apply power. A half-seated jumper or a rail powered from the wrong
end can otherwise look like a component fault.

## Worked examples: hazard, stored energy, and loading

We now put arithmetic on the three predictions, following the book's discipline
every time: **estimate → calculate → check units and limits → interpret.**

### Worked example: contact current at a 24 V bench node

*Estimate.* A bench node sits at 24 V DC. Dry-finger contact resistance is of
order tens of kilohms in this deliberately simplified screen, so the body of
@fig-shock-path becomes $R_\text{body}$ across the node. Ohm's law then suggests
a sub-milliampere result. State that expectation before computing; do not test it
by contact [@iec60479].

*Calculate.* Take a mid-range dry-skin path $R_\text{body} = 40~\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
I = \frac{V}{R_\text{body}} = \frac{24~\text{V}}{40\times10^{3}~\Omega}
  = 6.0\times10^{-4}~\text{A} = 0.60~\text{mA}.
$$

*Check.* Units: V/Ω = A. ✓ Revealing variation: reducing the illustrative path
value from 40 kΩ to 1 kΩ raises the screen to 24 mA, within the
several-to-tens-of-milliamperes band where painful contraction and impaired
release become possible. The answer is *conditional on the assumed path*,
exactly as the source–path–target picture warns.

*Interpret.* Under the stated dry-contact assumption the result is below the
approximate perception scale, while the 1 kΩ variation gives 24 mA. The large
change shows why skin impedance is not a design control and why intentional
contact is never a test method. The approved source voltage, current limit,
available energy, insulation, and procedure establish the bench boundary. This
calculation also omits stored energy and fault current, which must be bounded
separately.

### Worked example: current-limited short circuit

*Estimate.* A 3.3 V rail should draw ~150 mA when healthy. You set the supply's
current limit to 250 mA. A student accidentally shorts the rail to ground. Without
a limit, how bad is it; with the limit, how bad is it?

*Calculate.* Without a current limit, the fault current cannot be known from the
3.3 V label alone; the source's output relation and the complete path impedance
are required. To expose the scale, suppose an **illustrative stiff source** can
initially sustain 3.3 V into 30 mΩ of total source, lead, contact, and fault-path
resistance. The first-order estimate is
$I=3.3~\text{V}/0.030~\Omega=110$ A and source-terminal power is about 360 W,
distributed among the path elements according to their resistances. A real
source may current-limit, fold back, or fail before reaching that extrapolation.
*With* the 250 mA limit, the supply leaves constant-voltage mode and reduces its
output voltage to regulate the current. Its delivered terminal power is bounded
by
$P \le V_\text{set}I_\text{lim}
=3.3~\text{V}\times0.25~\text{A}=0.83$ W. This is a bound rather than an
equality because the output voltage across a low-resistance fault collapses.

*Check.* Units: V·A = W. ✓ The limiting behavior is correct: as the short
resistance $\to 0$, the limited supply holds *current* fixed and lets *voltage*
fall, so power stays bounded.

*Interpret.* Under the illustrative comparison, current limiting converts an
open-ended extrapolation into a bounded source-port event with a visible mode
change. Local contact heating, stored energy elsewhere, and a supply's transient
response still require separate checks. This is why the voltage and current
limits are set and verified before connection.

### Worked example: stored capacitor energy after disconnect

*Estimate.* A board holds a $C = 1000~\mu\text{F}$ bulk capacitor charged to 30 V
when you switch off (@fig-capacitor-bleed). Does the stored energy still require
a controlled discharge? Using rough numbers,
$E=\tfrac12 CV^2\sim\tfrac12(10^{-3})(10^3)=0.5$ J. That energy remains
available to the discharge path, so the path and its component ratings still
matter after disconnection.

*Calculate.* Precisely,
$E=\tfrac12(1000\times10^{-6}~\text{F})(30~\text{V})^2 = 0.45~\text{J}$, still
present *after* the supply is unplugged. If a 1 kΩ bleed resistor is fitted, the
voltage decays with $\tau=RC=(10^3~\Omega)(10^{-3}~\text{F})=1$ s, so after
$5\tau = 5$ s the ideal prediction is $30\,e^{-5}\approx 0.20$ V. With no
characterized discharge path, the decay time is unknown and must be verified.

![Stored energy outlives the switch. After switch $S$ opens the source is gone, but capacitor $C$ keeps its voltage $v_C$ and discharges through the characterized bleed resistor $R_\text{bleed}$, with time constant $\tau=R_\text{bleed}C$. Without a known path, the discharge interval cannot be assumed.](../../../build/circuits/f01-capacitor-bleed.png){#fig-capacitor-bleed fig-alt="Source V_S charges capacitor C through switch S; a bleed resistor R_bleed is connected permanently across C, and test point TP1 marks the capacitor voltage v_C. With S open, C discharges through R_bleed."}

*Check.* Units: F·V² = (C/V)·V² = C·V = J. ✓ And $RC$ has units of seconds, as the
dimensional check earlier confirmed.

*Interpret.* The 0.45 J remains available after disconnect and can produce an arc,
damage a tool or component, and cause a startle response if released through a
low-resistance path. Higher voltage or capacitance can raise both stored energy
and contact hazard sharply. This is the concrete meaning of step 3 of the
de-energizing sequence: **prove it is discharged; do not assume it.** It is also
a first look at the RC transient developed rigorously in
[F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd).

### Worked example: voltmeter loading of a divider

*Estimate.* A 9 V source drives a divider of $R_1=R_2=1~\text{M}\Omega$ (the
circuit of @fig-divider-loading); the ideal mid-node is 4.50 V. Your DMM has
$R_\text{in}=10~\text{M}\Omega$. From @eq-loading you expect a *few percent low*.

*Calculate.* The node's Thévenin resistance is
$R_\text{th}=R_1\Vert R_2 = 0.5~\text{M}\Omega$. The fractional loading error is

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V} = -\frac{R_\text{th}}{R_\text{th}+R_\text{in}}
   = -\frac{0.5}{0.5+10} \approx -4.8\%,
$$

so the meter should read about $4.50~\text{V}\times(1-0.048)=4.29$ V. (Computed
the long way — replace $R_2$ by $R_2\Vert R_\text{in}=0.909~\text{M}\Omega$ and
redo the divider — you also get 4.29 V, confirming the shortcut.)

*Check.* Units: a ratio of resistances is dimensionless. ✓ Limit: if
$R_\text{in}=10~\text{G}\Omega$ (an electrometer), the error is $-5\times10^{-5}$,
i.e. negligible. The higher input resistance reduces loading.

*Interpret.* The 0.21 V discrepancy is **not explained by noise or a component
fault**; within the stated equivalent it is a predictable systematic effect of
the intrusion. The calculated correction is an estimate, and uncertainty in
$R_\text{th}$, $R_\text{in}$, and the indication remains part of the corrected
result. You can apply that correction or reduce it by
measuring high-impedance nodes with a higher-$R_\text{in}$ instrument or a
buffer. What you must *never* do is report 4.29 V as the divider's true node
voltage without noting that your meter pulled it there.

## Prediction–measurement reconciliation

A prediction becomes evidence only after we test it. Example D gives a
controlled comparison, so we use it as the chapter's worked test:
**predict, measure, compare within uncertainty.** The physical version appears
in Lab **L01**. Here we focus on the structure of the record because the way we
report a result affects what another person can verify.

The following configuration and observations are **synthetic and illustrative**.
They show the form of a reproducible record and an uncertainty-aware comparison,
but they do not qualify a claim about physical hardware. An authentic record in
Lab **L01** must identify the actual instrument, range, loading, calibration state,
raw observations, operating conditions, and uncertainty basis as required by
[T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd).

- Source: illustrative bench supply setting of 9.00 V with a 10 mA current limit.
- Divider: $R_1, R_2$ nominally 1 MΩ ±1 %, with synthetic resistance
  observations of 0.998 MΩ and 1.003 MΩ.
- Meter: illustrative DMM on its DC-voltage range, with 10 MΩ input resistance,
  1 mV resolution, and an assumed accuracy limit of ±(0.5 % of reading +
  2 counts). The table's ± intervals are illustrative expanded uncertainties
  $U$ with coverage factor $k=2$; they are teaching assumptions, not a calibration
  claim or a direct conversion of a datasheet limit.

| Test point | Calculated prediction | Synthetic observation | Illustrative comparison |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-source voltage $V_S$ | 9.00 V (set) | 9.00 V ± 0.05 V | ✓ |
| Ideal mid-node (no meter) | 4.50 V | — (unobservable directly) | — |
| Mid-node, DMM loaded | 4.29 V | 4.28 V ± 0.03 V | ✓ within $U$ |
| Loading error $\Delta V$ | −0.21 V | −0.22 V ± 0.06 V | ✓ |

The synthetic mid-node interval, $4.28 \pm 0.03$ V, contains the loaded
prediction of 4.29 V. It does not contain the 4.50 V prediction that ignores
meter loading. This comparison shows how equation, schematic, and observation
can be reconciled using an explicit reference direction: red lead on the
mid-node and black lead on the supply return. Only the aligned physical lab can
establish that agreement for real hardware
[@horowitz2015art].

A short, dependency-free script makes the model reproducible and its parameters
visible rather than hidden — the book prefers recoverable source to a screenshot:

```python
# f01_bench_checks.py — parameterized checks for the F01 worked examples.
# Standard library only; run with:  python3 f01_bench_checks.py
def parallel(a, b):
    return a * b / (a + b)

def body_current(voltage_V, body_ohm):
    "Energy idea: estimate shock current for a contact path."
    return voltage_V / body_ohm

def cap_energy(C_farad, V_volt):
    "Energy idea: energy still stored after power-off."
    return 0.5 * C_farad * V_volt**2

def bleed_time(R_ohm, C_farad, n_tau=5):
    "Energy idea: time for a bleed resistor to (nearly) discharge C."
    return n_tau * R_ohm * C_farad

def divider_loaded(Vs, R1, R2, Rin):
    "Intrusion idea: true divider node vs. the value a finite-Rin meter reads."
    ideal  = Vs * R2 / (R1 + R2)
    loaded = Vs * parallel(R2, Rin) / (R1 + parallel(R2, Rin))
    return ideal, loaded, (loaded - ideal) / ideal

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(f"A: 24 V / 40k dry skin -> {body_current(24, 40e3)*1e3:.2f} mA")
    print(f"C: energy in 1000uF @ 30 V -> {cap_energy(1000e-6, 30):.2f} J")
    print(f"C: 5*tau bleed, 1k*1000uF -> {bleed_time(1e3, 1000e-6):.1f} s")
    ideal, loaded, err = divider_loaded(9.0, 1e6, 1e6, 10e6)
    print(f"D: ideal {ideal:.3f} V, loaded {loaded:.3f} V, error {err*100:.1f}%")
# Expected output:
#   A: 24 V / 40k dry skin -> 0.60 mA
#   C: energy in 1000uF @ 30 V -> 0.45 J
#   C: 5*tau bleed, 1k*1000uF -> 5.0 s
#   D: ideal 4.500 V, loaded 4.286 V, error -4.8%
```

Change a number — the body resistance, the input impedance, the capacitor — and
the model tells you the new consequence before the bench does. That is the loop
this book asks you to run on every claim.

## Failure modes in bench reasoning

A working idea earns trust by naming the conditions that break it. Three
misconceptions cause a large share of real bench damage, and one worked diagnosis
shows how to tell an intrusion from a fault.

**"Low voltage is automatically safe."** False. The current-limited-source
example showed that delivered power depends on the complete source and fault
path, while the capacitor example showed that stored energy remains after
disconnect. A low voltage can still heat a conductor, damage a component, cause
a burn, or produce a startling arc when source impedance is small or stored
energy is appreciable. "Low voltage" narrows one hazard; it does not remove the
need to bound current, energy, temperature, and the return path.

**"The meter shows the undisturbed circuit value."** It may not. The meter loads
the node, its bandwidth attenuates fast signals, its leads receive interference,
and a shared return can create an additional voltage. A reading directly
describes the circuit-plus-instrument system. To infer the undisturbed circuit
value, we must either show that loading is negligible or correct for it.

**"Units do not affect the result."** They do. In 1999 NASA's Mars Climate
Orbiter was lost because one team's software produced an impulse in
pound-force-seconds while the navigation software consumed it as newton-seconds; a
factor of 4.45 accumulated silently until the spacecraft entered the Martian
atmosphere at the wrong altitude and was lost [@nasa1999mco]. A dimensional
check would have exposed the mismatch between the two software interfaces.

::: {.callout-caution title="Worked diagnosis — intrusion or fault?"}
You measure the Example-D mid-node and read 4.28 V, not the 4.50 V predicted
when meter loading is ignored. Is $R_2$ lower than expected, or is the meter
loading the node? We can separate the hypotheses because they make different
predictions:

- **Loading** predicts the error scales with the meter: repeat with a
  higher-input-resistance instrument (or a unity-gain buffer) and the reading
  should climb toward 4.50 V.
- **A low $R_2$** predicts the error is *independent* of the meter and that a
  de-energized resistance measurement of $R_2$ reads low.

Swap to a 10 GΩ-input electrometer: the node reads 4.500 V. Hypothesis
"loading" survives; "fault" is refuted. The 0.22 V was the intrusion, exactly as
@eq-loading predicted. This is the whole method of
[F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd) in miniature: *isolate a cause by
finding evidence that separates it from the alternatives*.
:::

## Bench safety and measurement plan

Engineering is deciding under constraints and defending the choice. The decision
this chapter equips you to make is the **bench safety-and-measurement plan** for a
first investigation — the artifact that turns "let's probe it and see" into a
result another person would accept.

*Requirements (measurable, declared before power).*

- Work uses an approved source no higher than 24 V DC and remains within the
  locally defined current and available-energy limits.
- Set the current limit to the lowest value above the bounded healthy-current
  demand, including documented startup, tolerance, and transient needs; record
  the chosen margin rather than applying an unexplained universal factor.
- Every measured node's $R_\text{th}$ estimated in advance; instrument chosen so
  the loading error of @eq-loading is $< 1\%$, or the error is corrected and
  reported.
- Stop conditions written down: an unplanned current-limit indication, any
  reading outside the predicted range by more than its uncertainty, measured
  temperature or rise rate beyond a predeclared limit, odor, smoke, unexpected
  sound, discoloration, or unstable operation.
- A record sufficient for reproduction: supply settings, part values as
  *measured*, instrument model and ranges, raw readings with uncertainty.

*Alternatives and trade-offs.* A **higher current limit** lets a hungry circuit
settle faster and avoids nuisance trips, but widens the energy a fault can deliver
— speed traded against safety margin. A **direct DMM** is quick but loads
high-impedance nodes; a **buffered/high-$R_\text{in}$ probe** reduces that
loading but adds offset and bandwidth limits. Choose the instrument for each
node rather than once for the whole session.

*Acceptance test.* The plan is accepted when a dry run reproduces a characterized
reference value—such as the loaded 4.29 V mid-node of Example D—within its stated
uncertainty, and a rated test load requests more than the set current so the
supply enters current regulation without a direct short. Record the load rating,
indicated current and voltage, and source state. If the reference or limit
behavior cannot be reproduced, investigate the plan and apparatus before testing
the unknown circuit.

*Limit of the claim.* This plan qualifies **current-limited, extra-low-voltage
bench investigation only.** It is explicitly *not* a method for mains, stored
high-energy, or any hazardous system, and passing its acceptance test authorizes
nothing beyond the energy level and apparatus it names. The stated limit keeps
the engineering claim aligned with the evidence.

## Bench habits for later chapters

Carry three habits into every later chapter:

- **Before power, bound the energy.** Trace where energy can go, then set voltage
  and current limits.
- **During analysis, carry the units.** Check dimensions and distinguish a
  calculated value from a displayed value.
- **During measurement, include the instrument.** Ask how the instrument changed
  the node and interpret the reading as part of the joined system.

Record the configuration and reasoning so that another person can follow them.

Each of these three ideas is the seed of a later chapter, and the chapter is
deliberately the root of the book's dependency graph — it has no prerequisites and
almost everything depends on it:

- the **quantities** you agreed to reason in — charge, voltage, current, and their
  reference directions — are defined properly in
  [F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd);
- the **Ohm's-law and loading** estimates used here become the source-and-load
  theory of [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd);
- the **energy and power** bounds of Examples B–C grow into the power-and-thermal
  budget of [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd);
- the **uncertainty and intrusion** discipline is the whole subject of
  [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd), the pivot of the part; and
- the **record** you keep is formalized in
  [T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd).

That chain — F01 → F02 → F03 → F06 — is the *measurement spine* of Part I (see the
[part introduction](index.qmd)): it reaches a *trustworthy number* as early as
possible, because every later chapter quietly assumes you can produce one. You now
hold the habits that make the rest of the book safe to read on a real bench.

## Exercises

Work these with the estimate → calculate → check → interpret loop, and state the
assumptions and units every time. Full solutions and live-assessment variants are
maintained outside the public book; a few self-check answers are given so you can
calibrate.

### Quick check

One best answer each; the key follows. These take a minute and surface the most
common misconceptions before you invest in the longer problems.

1. Electrical safety is expressed first as a limit on
    a. voltage alone;
    b. the current through the body, and how long it flows;
    c. the source's internal resistance;
    d. the power factor.
2. You set a bench supply's current limit just above the healthy current, and a
   short then occurs. The supply
    a. delivers unlimited current until a fuse blows;
    b. raises its voltage to force current through the short;
    c. leaves constant-voltage mode, drops its output, and bounds the fault;
    d. shuts down permanently and must be replaced.
3. A resistor marked "1 mΩ" and one marked "1 MΩ" differ by a factor of
    a. $10^{3}$;
    b. $10^{6}$;
    c. $10^{9}$;
    d. nothing — the markings mean the same thing.
4. A 10 MΩ-input DMM measures the mid-node of a $2\times1~\text{M}\Omega$ divider.
   Compared with the ideal 4.50 V, the reading is
    a. higher than 4.50 V;
    b. lower than 4.50 V;
    c. exactly 4.50 V;
    d. unpredictable in sign.
5. A meter displays "4.286 V" but the measurement's uncertainty is ±0.05 V. The
   honest report is
    a. 4.286 V;
    b. (4.29 ± 0.05) V;
    c. 4 V;
    d. (4.286 ± 0.001) V.
6. "Powered off" guarantees a board is safe to touch. This is
    a. always true;
    b. true unless the board has capacitors, which can retain a hazardous charge
       until proven discharged;
    c. true one second after switch-off;
    d. true only for mains-powered boards.

*[Key: 1-b, 2-c, 3-c, 4-b, 5-b, 6-b. Each maps to a section above: 1 and 6 to
*Energy paths and hazard control*, 3 and 5 to *SI units and quantitative
discipline*, 4 to *Instrument loading and reference directions*, and 2 to the
current-limit habit.]*

**Retrieval and reasoning**

1. State, in your own words, why electrical *safety* is expressed as a limit on
   current (and duration) rather than on voltage alone. Name the approximate
   perception, let-go, and fibrillation thresholds [@iec60479].
2. List the five steps of the de-energizing sequence [@en50110] and explain why
   step 3 (verify absence of voltage) can never be skipped, even after step 1.
3. A rail is labelled "5 V." What three questions must you ask before that label
   is unambiguous? (Hint: DC? peak? RMS? referenced to what?)

**Estimation**

4. Estimate the current through your body if you grip both electrodes of a 12 V
   battery with (a) dry hands (~50 kΩ) and (b) salt-water-wet hands (~1.5 kΩ).
   Which, if either, would you perceive? *[Self-check: ≈ 0.24 mA and ≈ 8 mA.]*
5. A ¼ W, 220 Ω resistor is placed directly across a 12 V supply by mistake.
   Estimate its power dissipation and state whether it survives. By what factor is
   the rating exceeded? *[Self-check: ≈ 0.65 W, ~2.6× over rating — it will
   overheat.]*
6. Estimate the energy stored in a 470 µF capacitor charged to 50 V, and the
   $5\tau$ discharge time through a 2.2 kΩ bleed resistor.

**Derivation and units**

7. Starting from the units of the volt, ampere, and farad, show from first
   principles that the farad-volt-squared product $\tfrac12 CV^2$ has units of
   joules.
8. Derive @eq-loading from a two-resistor divider by replacing the lower resistor
   $R_2$ with $R_2 \Vert R_\text{in}$, and show that for $R_\text{in}\gg R_\text{th}$
   the fractional error reduces to $-R_\text{th}/R_\text{in}$.

**Data interpretation**

9. A datasheet quotes a DMM accuracy of "±(0.5 % of reading + 2 counts)" on a
   range with 1 mV resolution. For a displayed reading of 4.286 V, compute the
   accuracy-band half-width and write the result as a value with uncertainty and a
   sensible number of significant figures.
10. You measure a node three times: 3.301 V, 3.298 V, 3.303 V. Distinguish the
    *resolution*, the *scatter* (an A-type uncertainty), and any *loading* you
    would still need to consider separately before quoting a final value
    [@jcgm2008gum].

**Debugging**

11. A colleague measures the mid-point of a 2 × 4.7 MΩ divider from a 10 V source
    and reports 3.4 V, "so a resistor must be out of spec." Using @eq-loading with
    a 10 MΩ meter, compute what an *ideal-part* divider would actually read on
    that meter, and decide whether the data support the fault claim or an
    intrusion. Then design one measurement that discriminates the two.
12. A breadboard circuit works when probed and fails when the probe is removed.
    Give two mechanisms — one an energy or return-path effect, one a measurement
    intrusion (e.g. an intermittent contact the probe happens to complete; a
    ground path the probe supplies) — and a de-energized check for each.

**Open design**

13. Write a one-page bench safety-and-measurement plan (requirements, current
    limit, per-node instrument choice, stop conditions, and record format) for
    measuring the steady current and three node voltages of an unfamiliar 3.3 V
    sensor board. State the limit of the claim your plan can support.
14. Propose an acceptance test that would convince a skeptical reviewer that your
    plan in Exercise 13 actually bounds the fault energy and keeps every loading
    error below 1 %. What single piece of evidence would *falsify* it?

## Connections

Chapter ID: `F01`. Prerequisites are none — this chapter is the root of the book's
dependency graph — and downstream course mappings are maintained in
[`chapters.toml`](../chapters.toml). The [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) provides
reader-facing routes and the practice spine; this chapter opens the **measurement
spine** (F01 → F02 → F03 → F06) described in the
[Part I introduction](index.qmd).

The aligned practical evidence is **Lab L01** (bench safety and instrument
qualification), and the integrating milestone is **Project M1** (a qualified
energy-and-light measurement), which binds F01–F06 with reproducible records
([T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd)). Lab and project IDs denote
*evidence goals*, not permission to perform an activity: the safety level,
apparatus, stop conditions, and local authorization must be defined before any of
this becomes deliverable, and reading this chapter authorizes nothing beyond the
current-limited, extra-low-voltage boundary stated at its head.

## References

Claims in this chapter are cited at the point of use and prefer primary sources:
physiological current thresholds and the safe de-energizing sequence rest on the
relevant IEC and CENELEC standards [@iec60479; @en50110]; instrument and
laboratory-equipment safety, including measurement-category ratings, on
IEC 61010-1 and workplace electrical-safety practice [@iec61010; @nfpa70e]; the SI
units, the ampere's 2019 redefinition, and their correct use on the defining
brochure and the NIST guide [@bipm2019si; @nist811]; the vocabulary of
measurement and the expression of uncertainty on the VIM and the GUM
[@jcgm2012vim; @jcgm2008gum]; and the bench-first working method on the standard
practical reference [@horowitz2015art]. The complete, growing
[bibliography](../references.qmd) collects these. None of them, and neither this
book, substitutes for a datasheet, a governing standard, or supervised laboratory
training.
